title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
short_title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people affected by natural disasters to get more time to file their taxes. Right now, only disasters declared by the federal government can lead to tax deadline extensions, but this bill would let state governors ask for extensions when they declare disasters in their states too. The bill also gives people more time by extending the automatic extension period from 60 days to 120 days for certain taxpayers affected by disasters. This includes people who live or work in disaster areas, relief workers helping with disasters, and people who were hurt or killed in disasters.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Modification of Rules for Postponing Certain Deadlines by Reason of Disaster
sections.text: This section changes the tax rules about delaying tax deadlines when disasters happen. First, it gives the Secretary of the Treasury new power to delay federal tax deadlines when a state governor declares a disaster. The governor of any state or the mayor of Washington DC can ask in writing for this help. The Secretary can then treat the state disaster the same way as federal disasters and give people more time to file their taxes. A qualified state declared disaster means any natural disaster like hurricanes, tornadoes, storms, floods, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, landslides, mudslides, snowstorms, or droughts. It also includes fires, floods, or explosions no matter what caused them. The governor or mayor must decide that the damage is serious enough to need this tax relief. The word state includes Washington DC, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands. Second, this section changes how long certain tax deadline extensions must last. It increases the required extension time from sixty days to one hundred twenty days. This change applies to all disaster declarations made after this law becomes effective.
